start.text:
The deep stacks give up the founder's design a drawer at a time, and the design, read whole, arrives at a conclusion the household has been circling for four hundred kilometres: the mooring cannot be broken into. It can only be entered.

The as-builts are unambiguous. The shaft under the yard is not a door with a lock; it is a system with a member. The seals answer the wardkey, the wardkey answers the holder, and the holder is authenticated not by anything he carries but by everything he is — gait, voice, pulse, the whole signature of a specific nervous system, checked continuously, at depth, by instruments poured into the concrete itself. 'The building recognizes you or it entombs you,' Osy summarizes, flat, over the spread drawings. 'There's no picking that. There's only being you — provably, fluently, at every layer. Which brings us to the problem underneath the problem.' She taps the interface protocols, the thick annotated folders in the machinist's economical hand. 'The mooring's inner systems talk interface. The chair's language. You spoke it for eight years, and the eight years are exactly what was taken from you. If you walk in there as you are, you're a native speaker with amnesia carrying a phrasebook. So.' She looks up, and the lamplight finds the decision already made and waiting in her face. 'The stacks have a test cell. The chair's twin, shielded, offline, instrumented. The protocols include supervised re-familiarization. Before this household goes east, colleague, you are going to sit in that chair, and I am going to be on the other side of the glass with the abort line in my hand, and we are going to give you your own language back a session at a time.'

B, who has followed the drawings' migrations across the table for days with the patience of a court stenographer, looks from her to you, and does not wag, and does not object: the household's third opinion, reserving judgment, on the edge of the lamplight.

choices.text: Agree — but write the protocol first, together: session limits, abort criteria, the dog in the cell. If you're going back into your own head, you go instrumented.
Agree — and add one clause of your own before the drafting starts: the first anomaly, however small, surfaces the session. No heroics in your own head.

protocol_prep.text:
You write the protocol together, and it is the strangest document the household has produced: a treaty for visiting yourself.

Session limits: twenty minutes, hard, on the shielded cell's mechanical timer — 'machines keep promises,' Osy says, setting it, 'people keep intentions.' Abort criteria, listed and initialed: vitals off-corridor, speech in the wrong tense, and — her addition, written without comment — 'any sentence beginning with the word again.' The dog: in the cell, against the chair, non-negotiable; whatever B is below the weave, he is the household's finest instrument for the seams of you, and if something in the chair's language starts pulling, he will know it before the telemetry does.

The test cell itself, when you crank its lights, is a smaller, honester sibling of the horseshoe upstairs: one chair, its harness soft with age; a glass wall; a console whose layout your hands assess with a fluency that arrives before your eyes do — and that, precisely that, is the finding of the first afternoon, logged before you ever sit down. The hands know. Set a cold start sequence in front of you and your fingers walk it while your conscious mind reads labels two steps behind. Eight years live in you below the waterline of the theft. The interface protocols call this 'procedural retention, expected'; Osy's margin note beside the phrase reads, in minute vandal-proof: 'Expected by whom? Someone planned around what the taking couldn't take.'

On the second morning, harnessed, dog against your knee, glass wall lit, her voice in the earpiece running the checklist like a launch — 'session one, familiarization, twenty minutes, and colleague: the abort line is in my hand, and I have never once bluffed in my life' — you go under the headset, and the chair's language comes up around you like water remembering a swimmer.

choices.text: Session one: shallow, procedural, by the book — let the hands lead and the mind follow, and surface on the timer with the phrasebook a little less foreign.
Session one — and before the headset, hand her the abort line with ceremony, because instruments work better when the household can watch them being trusted.

sessions_montage.text:
The sessions become the campus's new liturgy, and for five days the liturgy holds.

Session by twenty-minute session, the language comes back the way rivers cut: not linearly — in captures. Whole idioms of the interface surfacing intact: state-lattice navigation, the reconciliation grammar, the queer beautiful notation for expressing a mind as differences against a reference — your notation, you keep discovering, cell after cell of it, the way a man might tour a city and keep finding his own name on the cornerstones. Between sessions you translate for the household: the mooring's systems, read through recovering eyes, resolve from cathedral-mystery into engineering — inspection regimes, consent gates, an architecture someone built with almost paranoid care to require its holder awake, informed, and willing at every layer. 'Whoever designed the entry,' you tell Osy on the fourth night, 'was afraid of exactly one scenario: himself, changed. It's defended against coercion, against impersonation, against — this is all through the design, like a watermark — against being used by someone who used to be its owner and isn't anymore.'

'Or,' she says, marking her place in the protocols with one finger, 'defended for the day the owner comes home not knowing himself, and needs the building to be certain on his behalf.' She looks at you across the lamp. 'Fear and hope compile identically, colleague. You of all people should know.'

The telemetry stays clean. The dog stays calm. The timer keeps its promises. And on the sixth day, in the nineteenth minute of session eleven, deep in the reconciliation grammar with your hands mid-phrase, the chair's language stops being a language you are remembering and becomes, without transition, warning, or seam, a language in which you are being remembered.

choices.text: [Cortex 5] Hold it. You built recall protocols for exactly this — anchor to the timer, name the intrusion as data, and ride the fragment with your instruments running instead of your fear.
It takes you. There is no holding this — the fragment closes over you like deep water, and the cell and the glass and her voice go very far away.

fragment_held.text:
You do the thing five ranks of discipline were bought for: you refuse the water the right to close.

Anchor: the timer's tick, found and held at the edge of hearing. Name it: intrusion, exogenous, timestamped — say the minute aloud, nineteen, and hear your own voice say it in the cell, thin but present, a surface maintained. And then, deliberately, like a diver paying out line, you let the fragment run — observed, instrumented, yours to record even if not yours to stop.

It is rain. That is the first data: rain on gravel, heavy, night rain, and the smell of wet concrete and cold steel, and a door — the hatch, you know it without ever having seen it open, the barn's roof hatch from beneath, rungs descending — and hands on the rungs that are your hands, wet, older, the knuckles skinned, and a weight in your chest so total, so structural, that your instrumented mind files it in awe even as it crushes: grief. Not fear of loss. Grief. Grief with a settled, furnished, load-bearing quality, grief that has moved in and hung its coat, grief for something already gone — and under the grief, worse than the grief, a fluency: the hands descending the rungs know this descent the way they knew the console, many times, routine, and a voice that is your voice, low, ragged, matter-of-fact, says into the rain-noise above:

'Again, then.'

And the timer fires, and the cell reassembles around its tick, and you are in the chair with your vitals shouting and your notes — you kept the notes; five ranks of discipline; the pencil never stopped — spelling out in wrecked shorthand the whole capture: RAIN. HATCH, FROM ABOVE. DESCENT ROUTINE — MANY PRIORS. GRIEF: ESTABLISHED, NOT ACUTE. VERBATIM: 'AGAIN, THEN.'

Through the glass, Osy's hand is frozen on an abort line she never had time to pull, and her face is asking the question. B is on his feet against your knee, pressed hard, staring up at you — at the seams of you — and the cell's cold air arrives in your lungs carrying the conclusion your instruments have already filed and your mouth has not yet agreed to say:

The fragment is not from before the Halfway. It was raining on the yard. The hatch was already built. The grief was already old. And the man on the rungs — you, provably, fluently, at every layer — was going down again.

choices.text: Tell her. Now, raw, notes in hand, before the fear can draft a kinder version — the treaty is the treaty, and this page is the heaviest one yet.
Bury it. Log 'intrusion, fragmentary, non-diagnostic' and stand down the session — she is decohering, the road east is already all cliff, and this stone does not need to go into her pack tonight.

fragment_taken.text:
The water closes, and you go where it takes you, because there was never a choice — only, later, a reckoning with how it felt to have none.

Rain. Rain on gravel, night rain, and the hatch from beneath — rungs, descending, wet steel in hands that are your hands and are older and are skinned at the knuckles — and a grief so settled, so furnished, so structural that it has stopped being an event and become an address: you live at the bottom of it; you have lived there long enough to stop noticing the stairs. The descent is routine. That is the obscenity at the center of it: the body knows this climb-down the way it knows the console, the way it knows a commute — many times, unremarkable, again — and a voice that is your voice says it aloud into the rain, low, ragged, administrative:

'Again, then.'

Then glass, light, the timer screaming its promise, hands on your shoulders — her hands; she has breached her own protocol and is in the cell — and B half across your lap with his weight pinning you to the present like a paperweight on a page in wind, and the world comes back in the wrong order: her voice first, saying your name in a register you have never heard it in; then the cold; then your own weeping, which has evidently been going on for some time, and which belongs — this is the part you will not be able to explain — to the man on the rungs. Not to you. You surfaced carrying someone else's tears, and they run down your face with the terrible fluency of long practice, grief with its coat already hung, and through them you hear yourself saying, over and over, in a voice you have no memory of learning: it's all right, it's all right, I go down, I always go down, someone has to hold —

And then it is gone. All of it, like water off glass, and you are a man in a chair in a cold cell with a dog on his lap and a colleague gripping his shoulders, empty-handed, wrung out, holding one verbatim line and the rain-smell already fading:

Again, then.

choices.text: Tell her. Everything the fragment left — the rain, the routine, the grief with its coat hung, the words — now, wrecked as you are, because the treaty was written for exactly this hour.
Bury it. She is already kneeling at your chair with fear in her face; give her 'a bad session, a fragment, nothing diagnostic' and carry the rungs alone — the household has cliffs enough ahead.

tell_her.text:
You tell her everything, in the cold cell, before the fear can draft a kinder version.

The rain. The hatch from beneath. The knuckles. The descent that the body knew as a commute. The grief that had stopped being an event and become an address. And the two words, verbatim, in your own voice: again, then. You give it to her the way the household gives everything now — raw, dated, uninterpreted — and she takes it standing, arms folded, tournament-still, and when you finish she is quiet for exactly as long as the finding deserves, and then she does the thing you told the fragment's tears about, the thing she has always done, the thing that is the whole reason the treaty works:

She makes it smaller by making it true.

''Again, then,'' she repeats. 'Grammar first. Again is iterative — it presupposes priors. Then is consequential — it accepts a schedule. Two words, and they contain a habit and a surrender both. Whoever said them —' her eyes come up — 'had done the thing before, and had stopped expecting it to be the last time. Now the timestamp. Rain on the yard, hatch built, descent routine: post-Halfway, by years of wear. Colleague, your stolen memories end at the Halfway. This isn't one of them. This is after. This is a memory of a man living your life on the far side of the theft — going down into that mooring, over and over, grieving, and the interface served it to you like mail, because —' and here even she has to stop, and turn her cup a quarter-turn, and choose the flat voice — 'because the chair thinks you're him. The building will recognize you or entomb you, we said. Here's the finding, and it goes in the file tonight, both hands: the recognizing has already started, and what it recognizes you as is a man who has made that descent many times and expects to make it again. The word for that isn't haunting.' She writes it, and turns the book to face you, and the lamplight holds the word steady between you like a piece neither player will touch: 'It's iteration.'

And then — because the household is the household — she closes the book, and pours the tea, and sits down next to you on the cold floor of the cell where the dog is, shoulder against your shoulder, two metres observed from the inside, and says, to the middle distance, in the voice she uses for exactly one person: 'Whatever number you are. However many rungs. You told me the same night. That's new, I'd stake the file on it — that's the variable somebody's been waiting forty-seven versions for somebody to change.' A pause. 'Made-up number. But you take my point.'

item.name: The Same Night
item.description: The fragment went into the file raw, dated, whole — the rain, the rungs, the grief with its coat hung, and 'again, then' — told to her in the cold cell before fear could draft a kinder version.

choices.text: Back to the stacks in the morning — because if 'again' has priors, then somewhere in the founder's records there is a ledger of them, and the household now knows what word to search for.
Sleep first — even load-bearing variables need rest — and in the morning, the stacks, and the founder's records, and the word the household now knows to search for.

bury_it.text:
You bury it, and discover what the digging costs.

'A bad session,' you tell her, in the cell, while the dog stares up at the seams of you. 'A fragment. Sensory, fragmentary, non-diagnostic. Rain, a door, vertigo. The protocols warned about bleed-through.' The sentences are all technically true, which is how you know exactly what you are doing, because you have watched a machine do it with bridges: nothing false enters the record; the record simply stops being the truth.

She watches you for a long moment through the glass-wall calm she keeps for triage, and then she nods, and logs your words verbatim — 'non-diagnostic, per subject' — and her pen hangs half a second over per subject before it moves on, and that half-second is the invoice, and you pay it in silence. The protocol stands down. The tea gets made. The evening performs itself: the board, the dog, the small liturgies, everything in its place, and you sit inside the household's warmth like a man sitting inside his own coat two sizes wrong, holding the rungs, the rain, the grief with its coat hung, and the two words — again, then — alone.

Alone it goes to bed with you. Alone it wakes at three, with the ring main humming, and runs the arithmetic you would not let her help you carry: the descent was routine. The grief was old. The man was you, at every layer, on the far side of the theft — which means the theft was not the end of your story but the middle of somebody's habit, and the habit's grammar is iterative, and its subject is you, and its object — the settled grief said so, the furnished grief, the grief that had stopped asking — its object was already lost before the rungs, every time, and there is exactly one thing in your life whose loss would furnish grief like that, and she is asleep six metres away with a dog against her back, decohering, trusting the file to be whole.

At four in the morning you take out the log and you write, in the space reserved for findings, in your own impatient hand, the only sentence the burial left you: 'The record is clean. The record is not the truth. Note the difference for the reader — he will need it.' You do not specify which reader. That is the other thing the digging costs: you have started leaving margins for a man you hope never has to stand where you are standing, and you recognize the habit, and you know now where you learned it.

item.name: Non-Diagnostic
item.description: The fragment logged as bleed-through, the truth carried alone: the rungs, the rain, the grief with its coat hung, and 'again, then.' Nothing false entered the record. The record simply stopped being the truth. Her pen hung half a second over 'per subject' — that was the invoice.

choices.text: Back to the stacks in the morning, carrying it alone — because if 'again' has priors, somewhere in the founder's records is a ledger of them, and you intend to find it before anyone else knows to look.
Sleep badly on it, alone with the margin you left for the reader — and in the morning, the stacks, and the ledger of 'again', found before anyone else knows to look.

ledger_door.text:
The search finds it on the second day, in the stacks' deepest aisle, behind drawings and protocols and eight years of beautiful dull work: a cabinet that is not a cabinet.

It stands at the aisle's end wearing the same institutional grey as everything else, and it is a door — steel, flush, unlabeled except for a works code, and the works code is MOORING/M — and where a lock should be there is a reader: a shallow tray, hand-shaped, poured in the same continuous concrete as the frame, with contacts of dull gold worn bright in the pattern of one specific palm. The as-builts, consulted, are laconic to the point of confession. RECONCILIATION LEDGER — MIRROR COPY. SYNCHRONIZED TO MOORING PRIMARY. ACCESS: HOLDER.

'A mirror of the mooring's own ledger,' Osy says, low. 'Here. Four hundred kilometres from the yard, under the lab where he was made. Every transaction, every purchase, every —' she looks at the reader's worn gold, the polish of one palm, many visits — 'every entry the Editor ever signed, synchronized west, into a room only the holder can open. Why. Why would he mirror his own books out here, at the far end of the longest lever —' and she stops, because she has answered herself, the way she always does, one clause too late to stop the sentence: 'Because the mooring reconciles. Whatever lives at the primary gets its versions merged, its records agreed, its yesterday edited to match its today. The mirror is the copy the mooring can't touch. He built himself an off-site memory, colleague. A notebook the machine can't wash. He built —' her voice arrives somewhere with no floor, and stands on it anyway — 'he built his own READ THIS FIRST, and he built it where only his own hand could open it, and there is exactly one hand on earth that answers that description, and I have watched it play chess badly for eleven years.'

B stands at the steel door, nose to the seam at its base, tail low and slow. His working tail. And the hand-shaped tray waits at the aisle's end with its gold worn bright by a palm that is, at every layer the building checks, provably, fluently yours.

choices.text: Put your hand in the tray. The ledger of 'again' is on the other side of that steel, and the household did not cross four hundred kilometres to stand in front of its own mail unopened.

end_ch11.text:
You put your hand in the tray.

The contacts are cold, and then they are warm, and the door does not click or grind — it considers. You feel the consideration run up your arm and through you the way the Warden's reading must have felt from outside: gait and pulse and the whole signature of a nervous system being asked, at depth, by patient instruments, the only question the building knows. Are you him. Are you still him. Are you him enough.

B presses against your leg. Osy's hand finds your shoulder — two metres, observed from the inside — and the stacks hold their institutional breath, and somewhere behind the steel a mechanism arrives at the end of a very long checksum, and decides.

The door opens on a small concrete room, and the room is full of paper, and the paper is full of you.

Shelves of bound fan-fold in the Continuum's cable-wax livery — but red, all of it red, transaction grammar floor to ceiling, SEQ numbers marching down the spines into the thousands. A plotting table with one chair, its seat worn in the pattern of one specific sitter. A wall of pinned pages in your own impatient hand — schedules, corrections, boxed question marks, a private war of marginalia conducted across years. And squared to the corner of the table, placed by a careful hand at the exact angle a man leaves a thing he intends a stranger to find first, a single binding, thinner than the rest, its cover bearing no works code and no sequence number — only five words, pressed hard, in handwriting you recognize the way you recognize your own face:

FOR 48. READ THIS FIRST.

Behind you, at the threshold the building would not have opened for any other palm on earth, Osy reads the cover over your shoulder, and the silence lasts three of her longest calculations, and then she says, in the flattest, most careful voice in her entire register, the finding that ends the chapter and begins whatever the household is now:

'Forty-eight. It's not a made-up number, colleague. It never was. Sit down in your chair. I'll make the tea. And then — both eyes, two skulls, at whatever it costs —' her hand tightens once on your shoulder, and holds — 'we are going to read your mail.'
